coredata.dc:title: A simple, fast and low-cost turn-on fluorescence method for dopamine detection using in situ reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiulan
Zhu, Yonggang
Li, Xie
Guo, Xuhong
Zhang, Bo
Jia, Xin
Dai, Bin
coredata.dc:description: A simple, fast and low-cost method for dopamine (DA) detection based on turn-on fluorescence using resorcinol is developed. The rapid reaction between resorcinol and DA allows the detection to be performed within 5 min, and the reaction product (azamonardine) with high quantum yield generates strong fluorescence signal for sensitive optical detection. The detection exhibits a high sensitivity to DA with a wide linear range of 10 nM–20 μM and the limit of detection is estimated to be 1.8 nM (S/N = 3). This approach has been successfully applied to determine DA concentrations in human urine samples with satisfactory quantitative recovery of 97.84%–103.50%, which shows great potential in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Resorcinol
Dopamine detection
Azamonardine
Turn-on fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis adsorption spectrums of resorcinol (Black), dopamine (Magenta), and products (Red). Fluorescence emission spectrum at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm (Blue), and fluorescence excitation spectrum (green) at the emission wavelength of 475 nm of azamonardine in aqueous solution with pH value of 9.0. The insert photographs show the fluorescence of azamonardine solution under natural light and UV lamp, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Effect of solution pH on PL intensity of azamonardine. The solution with different pH values was prepared by 10 mM phosphate buffer using pH meter.

(a) Fluorescence intensity is excited at 420 nm for DA reaction with resorcinol in absence of resorcinol, DA, oxygen or none under the same condition (RS, resorcinol), (b) fluorescence intensity as a function of reaction time with different alkali concentrations. The concentration of resorcinol and DA is 200 μM and 10 μM, respectively, the mixed solution pH is adjusted to 9.0 by HCl at the end of the reaction.
(a) Dependence of the PL intensity at 475 nm on DA concentration. (b) Relative fluorescence spectrum at different DA concentrations.

Effect of interferences on DA detection. The DA concentration is 10 μM and the concentration of other interferences is 1 mM, except for sucrose and tyrosine (500 μM), epinephrine and norepinephrine (5 μM). The PL intensity is normalized by the PL intensity of the product from resorcinol, DA reaction without interferences. DA, dopamine; Mix, mixture of all the interferences chemicals; VC, ascorbic acid; Gly, glycine; Glu, glutamic acid; Ala, alanine; Asp, aspartic acid; Lys, lysine; Glc, glucose; Suc, sucrose; UA, uric acid; Tyr, tyrosine; E, epinephrine; NE, norepinephrine.
DA reacting with resorcinol to synthesize azamonardine.
Comparison methods of sensitivity and response time for DA detection.

row.entry: Fluorescence quenching of dopamine in basic medium
Dopamine oxidation and reaction to ethylene diamine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of DA in human urine samples.

head.ce:title: A simple, fast and low-cost turn-on fluorescence method for dopamine detection using in situ reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, fast and low-cost method for dopamine (DA) detection based on turn-on fluorescence using resorcinol is developed. The rapid reaction between resorcinol and DA allows the detection to be performed within 5 min, and the reaction product (azamonardine) with high quantum yield generates strong fluorescence signal for sensitive optical detection. The detection exhibits a high sensitivity to DA with a wide linear range of 10 nM–20 μM and the limit of detection is estimated to be 1.8 nM (S/N = 3). This approach has been successfully applied to determine DA concentrations in human urine samples with satisfactory quantitative recovery of 97.84%–103.50%, which shows great potential in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a well-known neurotransmitter in central nervous, renal, hormonal and cardiovascular systems and plays a critical role in motor and cognitive functions [1,2]. Abnormal DA levels may result in several diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and anorexia [3]. Therefore, the detection of DA concentration is crucial for diagnosis of those diseases. The DA concentration in biological liquid ranges widely from 0.1 μM to 1.0 m M [4,5]. This requires the diagnostic instrument to be capable of sensing DA with a large dynamic range. At present, the most widely explored approaches to monitor DA levels are colorimetric method [6–8], High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [9], electrochemical analysis [10–14], and fluorescence spectroscopy [15–18]. Among those methods, the colorimetric method has relatively low sensitivity [7]. The HPLC approach relies on expensive equipment and specialized protocols [19]. Although the electrochemical approach has good sensitivity, its selectivity is limited by the interferences of the proximity oxidized molecules to DA, such as ascorbic acid and uric acid [20,21]. Attractively, the fluorescence-based DA detection technique has the advantages of high sensitivity, favorable selectivity and large detection range, and has attracted a great deal of attentions.

The fluorescence approach for DA detection mainly lies on fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence quenching strategy. Metal nanoparticles or quantum dots were mostly be utilized to enhance the fluorescence intensity through the strong interaction between the metal and catechol groups [18,20,22–25]. The theory of fluorescence quenching is based on the photoinduced charge-transfer process between the fluorescence materials and DA molecule, or the oxidation of DA to produce quinine [4,15–17,25–29]. However, the preparation of fluorescent materials like quantum dots, polymers and nanoparticles is complicated, time consuming, uneconomical, and sometimes not environmentally friendly. To address this issue, Yildirim and Bayindir [21] developed a straightforward technique to synthesize fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles in-situ in alkali solution for DA detection. This turn-on fluorescence detection is simple and has excellent selectivity and high sensitivity (LOD is 40 nM). However, the long response time and the unstable fluorescence of nanoparticles significantly limit its applications. So far, it has been a major challenge to find an ideal candidate material for DA detection with short response time and good fluorescence stability in real applications.

In this study, we develop a new simple, fast and sensitive turn-on fluorescence method for DA detection. Only the low-cost resorcinol is employed to react with DA in alkali condition to create fluorescence. The reaction product azamonardine has strong and stable fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity linearly increases to the DA concentration in a wide range of 10 nM–20 μM. The limit of detection is calculated to be as low as 1.8 nM. Furthermore, resorcinol has high selectivity for DA, and the detection is barely influenced by foreign molecules, including uric acid, ascorbic acid, amino acid, saccharides, urea, epinephrine and norepinephrine. Interestingly, the whole detection can complete within only several minutes without any pre-treatments. According to above excellent merits, it should be a promising method for DA detection in clinic diagnosis.

Dopamine hydrochloride, epinephrine, Tyrosine and norepinephrine(+)-bitartrate salt were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (U.S). Resorcinol, uric acid, ascorbic acid, aspartic acid, alanine, glycine, lysine, urea, glucose, sucrose, glutamic acid, hydrochloric acid, sodium carbonate, and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Shengao Co. and Guangfu Co. (Tianjing, China). All chemicals were used as obtained without further purification.

1.0 mL 200 μM resorcinol aqueous solution was mixed to 1.0 mL 200 μM DA aqueous solution with magnetic stirring. Instantly, 1.0 mL 50 mM Na2CO3 solution was added. After reacting 10 min, the obtained bright yellow solution was acidified to neutral by hydrochloric acid, and lyophilized. The freeze-dried powder was subjected to silica gel column chromatography eluted with petroleum ether-ethanol (3:1) to afford yellow solution. The solution was dried by rotary evaporation to get the orange red powder (azamonardine).

FT-IR spectrum was recorded from a BRUKER VERTEX 70 FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellet. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectrums were collected on a BRUKER AVANCE III HD 400 M superconducting NMR by dissolving azamonardine in DMSO-d 6. UV–vis adsorption spectrum of the azamonardine solution was recorded on a UV-3200 (Mapada) UV–vis spectrophotometer at room temperature. The fluorescence measurements were performed on a HITACHI F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a plotter unit and a 1 cm quartz cell.

1.0 mL DA solution with concentration range from 0 nM to 200 μM was mixed to 1.0 mL 200 μM resorcinol solution. Then 1.0 mL Na2CO3 (50 mM) solution was added. After stirring for 10 min, 0.3 M HCl was added to adjust the solution pH to 9.0. The fluorescence spectrum of the solution was recorded with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, and the photoluminescence (PL) intensity was collected at 475 nm.

A certain amount of DA and interferences were mixed in aqueous solution to obtain the DA concentration of 10 μM, the ascorbic acid, glycine, glutamic acid, alanine, aspartic acid, lysine, glucose, urea and uric acid concentration of 1 mM, the sucrose and tyrosine concentration of 500 μM, the epinephrine and norepinephrine concentration of 5 μM, respectively. Other parameters were the same as those in the sensitivity experiments.

In order to evaluate the detection of DA in clinical application, the standard addition method was used to analyze DA in healthy adult human urine samples. The urine samples were spiked with a certain amount of DA and analyzed. In this analysis system, the Na2CO3 solution with the concentration of 300 mM was employed to ensure that the reaction mixture's pH value was higher than 11.0. Briefly, 1 mL spiked urine sample was mixed with 1 mL resorcinol (1 mM), and then 1 mL Na2CO3 (300 mM) was added for 10-min reaction. The mixed solution was analyzed using fluorescence spectrophotometric after adjusting the pH value to 9.0 by HCl.

The FT-IR spectrum is shown in the supporting information (Fig. S1). The adsorption peak wavelength values are as follows: ν m (cm−1) 3418 (ν -OH, H2O), 2960, 2925, 2854 (ν C-H, Ar), 1631 (ν C
O), 1462 (ν -CH2), 1358 (δ C-OH, Ar), 1284, 1216 (ν C-O, Ar), 1181 (δ C-OH, Tertiary alcohol), 1111 (ν C-C, saturated hydrocarbon), 1032 (ν C-O-C, Ar), 803 (γ C-H, Ar 1,2,4 replace), 564 (ν N-H).

The UV–vis adsorption of DA, resorcinol and products, and the fluorescence emission and excitation spectrums of azamonardine are shown in Fig. 1 . It shows that DA and resorcinol has a strong adsorption peak at 280 nm and 274 nm, respectively. After reaction, the characteristic adsorption peak of DA is disappeared which indicates the DA has reacted completely. Additionally, a new strong adsorption peak at 420 nm demonstrates the generation of azamonardine [30]. The fluorescence emission spectrum displays a single emission peak at 475 nm when excited at 420 nm, and the peak shows no shift (blue or red) while excited from 260 nm to 460 nm (Fig. S5). These data indicate that azamonardine has a unique fluorescence property and monochromaticity.

The quantum yield of azamonardine at the excitation wavelength of 436 nm is calculated to be 72.6% using fluorescein as standard [31–33]. The high quantum yield and the visible excitation wavelength of photoluminescence from azamonardine allow it to be visible with naked-eyes under natural light or UV lamp (insert in Fig. 1). It should be noted that the fluorescence of azamonardine is very sensitive to pH as shown in Fig. 2 . The maximum fluorescence intensity is obtained at a pH value of 9.0. This reveals that the alkaline environment is beneficial for fluorescence generation of azamonardine. Fluorescence intensity of some fluorescents is tightly related to their ionization [34–36]. At optimized pH, the 11-OH group in azamonardine becomes anionic form resulting in strong fluorescence. This result is similar to reference [30].

Before performing the sensitivity assay, the possible impact of reactants and oxygen on detection fluorescence was investigated. Fig. 3 a shows neither resorcinol nor DA generates fluorescence in alkaline solution, which indicates the excess of resorcinol or DA has no effects on fluorescence detection. Additionally, under absolute anaerobic environment, the mixed solution is still colorless either by naked-eyes or UV lamp even after reacting 30 min. Furthermore, no fluorescence signal is observed (Fig. 3a (insert)), which reveals that oxygen is critical for this reaction.

To optimize the reaction conditions, we investigated the effect of alkali species and concentrations on fluorescence detection. Based on the pre-experiments, the reaction is fast and needs a short time to reach the equilibrium only when the pH value of the reaction mixture is higher than 11.0. So, two alkaline species (Na2CO3 and NaOH) and three concentrations (25 mM, 50 mM and 100 mM) were selected and explored. It is found that the reaction with Na2CO3 needs shorter time to equilibrate than that with NaOH at any concentrations (Fig. 3b). The possible reason is that the reaction is associated with oxygen concentration in solution [30]. NaOH tends to absorb CO2 from atmosphere, which may cause the decrease of dissolved oxygen in solution, and slows down the reaction. Therefore Na2CO3 is considered as a proper alkaline for this reaction. Even though the reaction is very fast so that the fluorescence is generated immediately when alkali is added to the reaction solution (Supporting video I), the reaction equilibrium time depends on Na2CO3 concentration. It is shorter with the increase of Na2CO3 concentration (9 min for 25 mM, 7 min for 50 mM, and 5 min for 100 mM). However, the fluorescence intensity for both 25 mM and 100 mM Na2CO3 concentrations is weaker than that with 50 mM Na2CO3. Therefore, 50 mM Na2CO3 is assumed to be optimum and selected for further study.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.09.023.

The following are the Supplementary data related to this article:
Video 1
The fast reaction between dopamine and resorcinol after alkali addition.
Video 1

To evaluate the selectivity of this method for DA detection, a range of potential interfering substances, such as uric acid, ascorbic acid, amino acids, saccharides, urea, epinephrine, and norepinephrine were investigated. The results in Fig. 5 show that all the interferences in the tested concentration (far higher than the physiological concentration) have no obvious effect on DA determination. It demonstrates that this method has high specificity for DA detection.

In real diagnosis applications, the DA concentration in urine can directly reflect the activity of the sympathoadrenal system. It is important to detect DA in urine for the diagnosis of pheochromocytoma and related diseases [40]. To demonstrate this capability, we used three human urine samples to investigate the performance of the current technique for DA detection. The experiments were carried out with known amount of DA added to urine samples to obtain different concentrations. The corresponding fluorescence intensity at 475 nm was recorded and compared to the values obtained in the absence of urine. The calculated results are summarized in Table 2 . Good recovery results (97.84%–103.50%) are obtained and the interferences in urine samples have little effect on DA detection. This indicates that this method is very promising in clinical application.

In summary, we have developed a new rapid method for DA detection with high sensitivity and selectivity by using the low-cost resorcinol. This method is extremely simple and does not need complicated processing step for sensor preparation. It is based on the turn-on fluorescence strategy through the reaction between resorcinol and DA. A high quantum yield of reaction product (azamonardine) allows the fluorescence signal to be easily detected. The fluorescence intensity greatly depends on DA concentration and the linear relationship is obtained in a wide range from 10 nM to 20 μM with the limit of detection as low as 1.8 nM. Furthermore, the detection exhibits high selectivity among other analogues such as uric acid, ascorbic acid, amino acids, saccharides, urea, epinephrine and norepinephrine, and has been proven to be capable of detecting DA in human urine samples. Therefore, this simple and fast approach is very promising for real diagnostic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (21264013, 21364010). X. Zhang also acknowledges CSIRO Manufacturing, Clayton, Melbourne Australia, for providing the support during her stay at the Microfluids and Fluid Dynamics Group.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
FT-IR spectrums, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, EI-Mass, fluorescence emission spectrums.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.09.023.
